name: Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam
title: Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (RA): The House of Islam
meta_description: The story of Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (RA), the quiet teenager whose house beside Safa became the first school of Islam, where the Prophet Muhammad taught and many companions accepted the faith.
keywords: Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam, Dar al-Arqam, house of Islam, first Muslims, sahaba stories, Banu Makhzum, the Firsts
lede: There is a companion the books barely describe. We have no sayings narrated from him. We have no scene of him speaking. What we have is a door, and a house behind it, where the future of a faith was quietly raised.
This is the story of Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him): a quiet teenager who gave his home to be the first school of Islam.

sections.h2: A boy from the wrong tribe
paras: Al-Arqam was very young when he believed. The narrations place him somewhere between twelve and sixteen years old, most often around fourteen. A boy of fourteen then was not a boy of fourteen now, but he was young by any measure, and he was quiet. That quietness is likely one reason so little of him survives in the records.

By one narration in al-Hakim, he was the seventh person to accept Islam. The numbers shift between accounts, because the message was being preached in secret and many kept their faith hidden. What is clear is that he was among the very first.

His tribe was Banu Makhzum. That is the tribe of Abu Jahl, who would rise to become the fiercest enemy of the Prophet ﷺ, a man some called the Pharaoh of this ummah. Banu Makhzum was the great rival of Banu Hashim, the Prophet's own clan. Al-Arqam belonged to the household of the opposition, and no one suspected him.

Al-Arqam had inherited a home from his father, and its location was everything. It stood right beside Safa, the hill where the people of Makkah climbed to make their announcements. A narrow alley ran from Safa to his door, set among the crowds of the city, so a person could slip inside without being noticed.

Think of who Al-Arqam was. A young Makhzumi who could walk freely through the streets, sit in the gatherings of Abu Jahl's own people, and draw no suspicion at all. No one watched him. No one worried about him. That is exactly what made the choice of his house so quietly brilliant.

The Prophet ﷺ made this house his gathering place. He would come every day to teach the small group of believers around him, most of them from the oppressed and the poor: Bilal, Suhayb, Ammar, Khabbab, and others. In a city watching for any sign of the new faith, this was the one room where it could breathe.

So much happened behind that door. Revelation came down to the Prophet ﷺ inside it. A great deal of the Quran was recited to a group of people for the first time in that house. It was here that Ammar ibn Yasir and Suhayb accepted Islam. It was here that Hamza and Umar (may Allah be pleased with them) took their shahada.

It was a school before there were schools, a place where the Quran was learned and the heart was shaped. The Prophet ﷺ did not only accept their faith and teach them how to pray. He built a bond among them, a community, a sense of who they now were.
It was in this house, the books tell us, that Allah revealed a verse of reassurance to the Prophet ﷺ and the few who stood with him:

ayah.text: Prophet, God is enough for you, and for the believers who follow you.

paras: We should not soften how frightening this was. Every time a believer stepped into that house, there was a chance of being discovered, and discovery could mean torture or death. Every time they stepped back out, there might be someone waiting with a sword or a spear.

When Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) once knocked on that door, those inside thought he had come to kill them. They thought they had been found out. And still, day after day, they came back. They had decided that this faith was worth the walk to the door.

Some of the scholars noticed a quiet echo here. Musa, peace be upon him, was raised in the house of Pharaoh. And here a young ummah was being raised in the house of a boy from the tribe of the Pharaoh of its own time. Allah was sheltering His message in the last place anyone would look.

Al-Arqam used the one advantage he had, his standing in Banu Makhzum, and spent it entirely on the Prophet ﷺ. He kept his faith private. He never made it public, never sought to be counted among the great names, never asked to be celebrated or propped up.

There was wisdom in his house beyond its hiding place. Even had he been discovered, Abu Jahl would not kill one of his own tribe, and there would be no boycott of the kind a Hashimite host would have brought. The Prophet ﷺ chose well, and Al-Arqam was content to be the unseen reason it worked.

He asked for nothing in return. He enjoyed being unknown in a city full of men like Abu Jahl, while behind his walls the Prophet ﷺ raised a generation. His name would forever be tied to that home, and that was enough for him.

Al-Arqam made the migration to Madinah with the Prophet ﷺ. There is a narration that the Prophet ﷺ granted him a home there, a sign of loyalty to the young man for what he had given. He fought every battle at the Prophet's side: Badr, Uhud, the Trench. He did not miss a single one.

He lived through the years of all four rightly guided caliphs and died in the time of Muawiyah (may Allah be pleased with him). We have no sayings to quote from him, no portrait to paint. He remained, from beginning to end, a very subtle presence.

His one wish, when he passed, was that his house should never be sold. It was a bond he kept to that place his whole life. And one can imagine the Prophet ﷺ, returning to Makkah in victory at the head of thousands, standing again on Safa where he had once been rejected, and looking just to the right of the hill, to where it all began.

lessons_intro: His story has almost no words in it. That is the point. A life can serve a great deal without ever being heard.

lessons.text: A teenager gave the early Muslims their safest ground. Age is not a reason to wait for a role in something good.
He never spoke, never sought credit, never made his faith public. He simply opened a door, and history turned on it.

lessons.bold: Use what you have where you stand.
lessons.text: His one advantage was his tribe and his house. He spent both on what mattered and asked for nothing back.
What was built in that room was not only knowledge but belonging. Our certainty is often strengthened the same way, among a few.

faq.a: An early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, from the tribe of Banu Makhzum. As a teenager he gave his house beside Safa to be the secret gathering place where the Prophet ﷺ taught the first Muslims, a place later remembered as the House of Islam.

faq.q: Why was his house so important?
faq.a: It stood next to Safa, reachable by a narrow alley among the crowds, so believers could enter unseen. Because Al-Arqam was from Abu Jahl's own tribe and kept his faith private, no one suspected it. The Prophet ﷺ taught there daily, and many companions accepted Islam inside it.

Dar al-Arqam means the House of Al-Arqam. It became known as the first school of Islam, where the Quran was taught and a community was formed. Many Islamic schools today carry the name in memory of it.

faq.q: What can we learn from Al-Arqam?
faq.a: That quiet, unrecognised service can change everything, that the young have a real role to play, and that faith is often nurtured best in small, sincere gatherings rather than on a public stage.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Most of us will never be the name on the list. We are the ones who open a door, who hold a space, who do the unseen work that lets something good take root. Al-Arqam shows that this is not a lesser part. The Prophet ﷺ raised an ummah in his house, and the young man was content to never be heard.

There is something to carry into an ordinary day here. Find the small advantage you have, the room you can offer, the gathering you can keep, and give it quietly, asking for nothing in return. The work that no one sees is still seen by the One who matters most.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 8:64). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

We have no sayings narrated from him.

No scene of him speaking.

What we have is a door, and a house behind it, where the future of a faith was quietly raised.

He was around **fourteen** when he believed.

A quiet teenager, one of the very first to accept Islam.

His tribe was **Banu Makhzum**: the tribe of Abu Jahl, the fiercest enemy of the Prophet ﷺ.

A boy from the household of the opposition. No one suspected him.

His house stood right beside Safa.

A narrow alley led to the door, hidden among the crowds. A believer could slip inside unseen.

Inside that house, the Prophet ﷺ taught the first Muslims every day.

Revelation came down there. Ammar, Suhayb, Hamza, and Umar took their shahada there.

Every time they stepped in, they risked being found out.

Every time they stepped out, a sword might be waiting.

Still, they came back. The faith was worth the walk to the door.

He never made his faith public. He never asked to be celebrated.

He spent his one advantage on the Prophet ﷺ, and was content to be the unseen reason it worked.

**Quiet service is still service.**

He simply opened a door, and history turned on it.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Some of the most important people in history are the quietest. Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was a teenager from Banu Makhzum, the tribe of Abu Jahl himself. No one suspected that the boy walking freely through Makkah was a believer.

He gave his house beside Safa to the Prophet ﷺ. Behind that door, the first Muslims learned the Quran, took their shahada, and became a community. It was the House of Islam, the first school of the faith. He never spoke a recorded word, never sought credit, and asked only that his home never be sold.

What would you have done in his place? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/al-arqam-ibn-abil-arqam

email.subject: The boy who gave his house to Islam
email.alt_subject: The first school of Islam was a teenager's home
email.preview: We have no sayings from him. Only a door, and what happened behind it.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a companion the books barely describe. We have no sayings narrated from him, no scene of him speaking. What we have is a door, and a house behind it.

His name was Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him), and he was very young when he believed, somewhere around fourteen. By one account he was the seventh person to accept Islam.

His tribe was Banu Makhzum, the tribe of Abu Jahl himself, the fiercest enemy of the Prophet ﷺ. And that is exactly what made him so useful. A young Makhzumi could walk freely through Makkah, sit among Abu Jahl's own people, and draw no suspicion at all. No one watched him.

He had inherited a house beside Safa, reachable by a narrow alley among the crowds. The Prophet ﷺ made it his gathering place. He came every day to teach the small group of believers around him. Revelation came down inside it. Ammar, Suhayb, Hamza, and Umar all took their shahada there.

We should not soften how frightening this was. Every time a believer stepped in, there was a chance of being discovered, and discovery could mean death. When Umar once knocked on that door, those inside thought he had come to kill them. And still, day after day, they came back.

Al-Arqam asked for nothing in return. He never made his faith public, never sought to be counted among the great names. He spent his one advantage on the Prophet ﷺ and was content to be the unseen reason it worked. His one wish, at the end, was that his house should never be sold.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss. Most of us will never be the name on the list. We are the ones who open a door, who hold a space, who do the quiet work that lets something good take root. That is not a lesser part.

May Allah be pleased with him, and gather us among the few in the company of the Prophet ﷺ.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: There is a companion the books barely describe. We have no sayings narrated from him, and no scene of him speaking. What we have is a door, and a house behind it, where the future of a faith was quietly raised.

Al-Arqam ibn Abil-Arqam (may Allah be pleased with him) was very young when he believed, somewhere around fourteen, and quiet by nature. He came from Banu Makhzum, the tribe of Abu Jahl, the fiercest enemy of the Prophet ﷺ. Because of that, no one suspected him. A young Makhzumi could walk through Makkah and sit among the opposition, and no one watched him.

He had inherited a house from his father, and its place was everything. It stood right beside Safa, reachable by a narrow alley among the crowds, so a believer could slip inside unseen. The Prophet ﷺ made it his gathering place and came every day to teach the small group around him, most of them poor and oppressed.

So much happened behind that door. Revelation came down inside it. A great deal of the Quran was recited to people for the first time there. Ammar and Suhayb accepted Islam in that house, and so did Hamza and Umar. It was a school before there were schools, a place where the Quran was learned and a community was formed.

We should not soften how frightening it was. Every time they stepped in, there was a chance of being found out, and that could mean death. Every time they stepped out, a sword might be waiting. When Umar once knocked, those inside thought he had come to kill them. And still they came back, because the faith was worth the walk to the door.

Al-Arqam asked for nothing in return. He never made his faith public, never sought to be counted among the great names, and was content to be the unseen reason it all worked. He made the migration to Madinah, fought every battle beside the Prophet ﷺ, and lived to old age. His one wish, at the end, was that his house should never be sold.
storybook.pullquote: He simply opened a door, and history turned on it.
storybook.reflection: Most of us will never be the name on the list. We are the ones who open a door, who hold a space, who do the quiet work that lets something good take root. Al-Arqam shows that this is not a lesser part. Give what you have, where you stand, and ask for nothing back. The work no one sees is still seen by the One who matters most.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
